section.title: The Two Ways
section.sectionRationale: An untitled wisdom psalm placed as the gateway to the whole Psalter. It teaches by contrast rather than narrative: the blessed man rooted in God's law against the wicked who are rootless chaff, a frame for reading every psalm that follows.
section.thesis: The blessed life is rooted delight in the LORD's law, fruitful and enduring like a tree by water, while the wicked life has no root and cannot stand in the judgment.
section.frameNote: Two portraits: the blessed man who delights in the law, pictured as a fruitful tree (vv1-3), and the wicked, pictured as chaff, with the LORD's verdict on both ways (vv4-6).

units.label: The blessed man
units.note: delight in the Law of the LORD

verses.text: {{ashre|Blessed}} is the man who does not walk in the counsel of the wicked, or set foot on the path of sinners, or sit in the seat of mockers.
But his delight is in the {{torah|Law}} of the LORD, and on His law he meditates day and night.
He is like a tree planted by streams of water, yielding its fruit in season, whose leaf does not wither, and who prospers in all he does.

units.label: The wicked and the verdict
units.note: chaff driven off by the wind

verses.text: Not so the wicked! For they are like chaff driven off by the wind.
Therefore the wicked will not stand in the judgment, nor sinners in the assembly of the righteous.
For the LORD guards the path of the righteous, but the way of the wicked will perish.

ashre.gloss: Blessed, happy, the fortunate condition of one who lives rightly before God. It is a pronouncement rather than a wish: this is the person to be envied. It opens both the Psalter, Psalm 1:1, and the Sermon on the Mount, Matthew 5:3-11, joining wisdom's blessedness to kingdom blessedness.

torah.gloss: Instruction, law, teaching, the LORD's whole revealed will for life, not merely a list of rules. The blessed man's delight and meditation rests here day and night, the mark of true wisdom, echoed at length in Psalm 119.

basic.context: Psalm 1 stands as the gateway to the whole Psalter. It is an untitled wisdom psalm, more a meditation than a prayer, that sets before the reader two ways: the blessed path of delight in God's law and the way of the wicked that ends in ruin. It teaches how to read everything that follows.
basic.hermeneutics: This is wisdom literature, not narrative or prophecy, so it teaches by contrast rather than by reporting an event: two paths, two destinies. People debate whether the righteous man pictured here is sinless; the psalm itself assumes ordinary believers who still delight in God's word rather than the perfectly flawless.
basic.originalLanguages: The psalm opens with ashrei, blessed, a pronouncement of true happiness, centered on torah, God's instruction, which the blessed one loves enough to meditate on day and night. The wicked, by contrast, are chaff, dry husks with no root and no weight, blown away at the first wind.
basic.typology: The tree planted by streams of water, always fruitful and never withering, points forward to Christ, the truly blessed man who perfectly delighted in His Father's law and was never uprooted, and by union with Him believers become fruitful trees themselves.
basic.christ: Jesus is the one man who never walked in the wicked's counsel, never stood in the way of sinners, never sat with mockers, whose whole life was delight in His Father's law. He is Psalm 1's blessed man in full, and all who are united to Him by faith share His fruitfulness and are spared the coming judgment.
basic.god: God is the quiet gardener behind this psalm: He guards the path of the righteous, and under His judgment the wicked cannot stand. He is named only once, but every verb of blessing and every verdict of the last day belongs to Him.
basic.crossReferences: Jeremiah 17:7-8 pictures the same tree planted by water. Matthew 7:24-27 echoes the two ways and two foundations. Galatians 5:22-23 describes the fruit that grows from a life rooted in the Spirit.
basic.oneStory: Psalm 1 opens the Psalter with the Bible's oldest choice: the way of life, rooted in God's word, or the way of death, rootless and blown away. Every psalm after this is sung by someone walking one of these two ways, and the whole Bible resolves the choice in Jesus, the truly blessed man who makes His people fruitful trees.

context.literary: An untitled wisdom psalm deliberately placed as the Psalter's frontispiece, built on a single sustained contrast between two ways of life, using nature imagery, a tree and chaff, rather than direct address to God.
context.historical: Wisdom psalms like this one stood alongside Israel's broader wisdom tradition in Proverbs, Job, and Ecclesiastes, teaching a two-ways theology familiar across the ancient Near East but grounding it specifically in torah, the LORD's own revealed instruction to His covenant people.
context.canonical: As the gate to the Psalter, Psalm 1 announces that the whole book of prayers and praises to follow is to be read as instruction for the blessed life. It is commonly read as a matched pair with Psalm 2, wisdom and kingship set side by side at the entrance to the book.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To set before the reader, at the very threshold of the Psalter, the stark difference between the blessed life of delight in God's law and the doomed life of the wicked, so that every psalm that follows is heard as a call to choose the first way.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes two outcomes rather than legislating specific behavior; the enduring prescription is the call to delight in God's word rather than take counsel from the wicked, not a formula guaranteeing earthly prosperity to every believer who reads Scripture.

debates.question: Does the tree's fruitfulness and prosperity promise material blessing to the righteous in this life?

views.label: A guarantee of earthly flourishing
views.case: The image of a fruitful, evergreen tree planted by water suggests tangible blessing follows delight in God's law.

views.label: A promise of spiritual fruitfulness and final vindication, not a material guarantee
views.case: Scripture elsewhere shows righteous sufferers, Job, the psalms of lament, and Christ Himself, so the prosperity described is ultimately spiritual fruit and vindication in the judgment, not an exemption from present hardship.

debates.bearing: Read alongside the Psalter's many laments, the promise is best understood as certain ultimate flourishing and vindication rather than a guarantee against present trouble.

study.originalLanguages: The psalm's opening word, ashrei, blessed, pronounces this person's true happiness before any reason is given. The reason follows: delight in torah, the LORD's instruction, meditated on day and night, using a verb, hagah, that pictures a low murmur of repeated recitation. The wicked are then pictured as mots, chaff, worthless husk with no weight or root, scattered at the first wind.

typology.type: The tree planted by streams of water, Psalm 1:3
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the truly blessed man whose life never withers, and all who are united to Him by faith, made fruitful trees themselves, Jeremiah 17:7-8, John 15:5.

study.christ: Jesus alone perfectly walked the blessed path: He never took counsel from the wicked, never stood in the sinner's way, never sat with mockers, and His whole life was unbroken delight in His Father's law. He is the tree that never withers, and He was cut down at the cross so that those united to Him, though we have walked in the wicked's counsel, are counted righteous and made fruitful trees ourselves, planted by the streams of His Spirit.
study.god: God is the unseen gardener of this psalm, the one who guards the path of the righteous and before whose judgment the wicked cannot stand. His care is patient and quiet, watering and guarding, but His verdict on the last day is certain and final.

crossReferences.note: The same picture of a tree planted by water, unafraid in drought, describing the one who trusts the LORD.
Jesus' own two ways: the wise man who builds on rock and the fool who builds on sand, echoing Psalm 1's contrast.
The fruit of the Spirit, the harvest that grows from a life rooted in God rather than in the flesh.

study.oneStory: Psalm 1 sets the whole Psalter, and in a sense the whole Bible, before a single choice: the blessed way of delight in God's word, fruitful and enduring, or the wicked way, rootless and blown away in judgment. Jesus Christ is the one who walked the blessed way perfectly, and all who trust Him are grafted into His life and made trees planted by streams of living water.

sermon.title: Delighting in the Law of God
sermon.why: Piper preaches through Psalm 1's contrast between the blessed man who delights in God's law and the wicked who are like chaff, drawing out what it means to actually delight in Scripture rather than merely obey it.

quiz.q: What is the blessed man's defining mark in Psalm 1:1-2?

options.text: He does not walk with the wicked, and his delight is in the LORD's law, which he meditates on day and night.
options.note: Both the negative avoidance and the positive delight define the blessed life.

options.text: He avoids sinners and mockers entirely.
options.note: True as far as it goes, but it omits the positive heart of the psalm, delight in God's law.

options.text: He works hard and prospers financially.
options.note: The psalm's prosperity is about fruitfulness under God, not a formula for wealth.

options.text: He never sins.
options.note: The psalm does not claim sinlessness; it describes a life oriented toward delight in God's word.

quiz.q: What image describes the wicked in verse 4?

options.text: Chaff that the wind drives away, with no root and no weight.
options.note: This is the psalm's sharp contrast to the deeply rooted, fruitful tree.

options.text: A tree that has stopped bearing fruit.
options.note: Close in spirit, but the actual image given is chaff, not a fruitless tree.

options.text: A stone that cannot be moved.
options.note: This misreads the image; chaff is light and blown away, the opposite of an immovable stone.

options.text: A shepherd who has lost his flock.
options.note: This image is not in the psalm at all.

quiz.q: What happens to the wicked in the judgment, according to verse 5?

options.text: They will not stand in the judgment, nor sinners in the assembly of the righteous.
options.note: The psalm states this plainly as the outcome of the wicked's rootless life.

options.text: They will eventually repent and be welcomed in.
options.note: A hopeful thought, but not what this verse states; the psalm's verdict here is exclusion.

options.text: They will be judged more leniently than the righteous.
options.note: The text says the opposite: they will not stand at all.

options.text: Nothing; the psalm has no final judgment.
options.note: Verse 5 explicitly describes a judgment and an assembly the wicked cannot enter.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament fulfill the picture of the blessed man?

options.text: Jesus Christ perfectly delighted in His Father's law and lived the blessed life in full, making His people fruitful trees by union with Him.
options.note: Christ fulfills the blessed man's pattern, and believers share His fruitfulness by faith.

options.text: Every believer automatically becomes sinless like the blessed man.
options.note: Union with Christ brings fruitfulness and standing, but not present sinlessness.

options.text: The blessed man is only a moral example with no connection to Christ.
options.note: This misses the deeper typological connection Scripture draws to Christ's own perfect obedience.

options.text: The New Testament never refers back to this psalm's imagery.
options.note: Jesus' own teaching on the two ways and Jeremiah's tree image both echo Psalm 1 directly.

quiz.q: What is the meaning of 'meditates' in verse 2?

options.text: A low, repeated murmuring or reciting of God's word, showing sustained, loving attention rather than a single reading.
options.note: The Hebrew hagah pictures ongoing, murmured reflection, not a single glance at Scripture.

options.text: Silent, purely mental reflection with no repetition.
options.note: Reflection is part of it, but the word specifically carries a sense of murmured repetition.

options.text: Memorizing the text once and moving on.
options.note: The picture is ongoing, day and night, not a single act of memorization.

options.text: A formal liturgical ceremony performed once a year.
options.note: This is not what the word or the verse describes.

questions: Psalm 1 begins with what the blessed man does not do before it describes what he delights in. Where do you need to step back from the counsel, path, or seat of those who scorn God, before you can delight in His word?

The blessed man meditates on God's law day and night, a picture of sustained attention, not a single reading. What would it look like for you to return to Scripture again and again this week rather than treating it as a box to check?

The tree is fruitful because it is planted by streams of water, not because it works harder than the chaff. How does this shape the way you think about spiritual fruit, as something that flows from being rooted, not from sheer effort?
Chaff has no weight and no root; it looks like part of the harvest until the wind comes. Where might you be relying on appearances of faith rather than genuine roots in God's word?

Jesus lived the blessed life perfectly on your behalf. How does knowing that your standing before God rests on His fruitfulness, not your own, change how you approach your own failures to delight in His law?

translationNotes.note: Ashrei, blessed, is grammatically plural in Hebrew, sometimes rendered 'oh, the blessednesses of' the one who lives this way, an intensified pronouncement of happiness rather than a simple wish.
Meditates renders hagah, a word that can mean to murmur, mutter, or growl low in the throat, describing the quiet, repeated recitation of memorized Scripture rather than silent study alone.
Chaff describes the light husk separated from grain at threshing and blown away by wind, a common ancient Near Eastern image for what has no lasting substance or value.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Psalm 1:1-6. No em or en dashes appear in this psalm's BSB text, and none are used anywhere in this file. Sermon link verified this session by fetching the Desiring God messages page for Psalm 1.
